返回本批次

海岸奇才队

United Wizards of the Coast

unitedwizardsofthecoast.com 作者 d4mi3n 分数 225 评论 209 2026/04/28 02:34
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 今天是万智牌历史上激动人心的一天！我们，Arena 团队的工人聚集在一起，成立了海岸奇才队 (United Wizards of the Coast) 工会 - CWA。上周，绝大多数符合条件的 Arena 工人签署了工会卡以承诺支持。今天早上早些时候，我们向 WOTC 领导层通报了我们成立工会的意图，并呼吁该公司自愿承认我们的工会：https://unitedwizardsofthecoast.com/letterSo, 下一步是什么？我们已经表明我们得到了 Arena 团队的大力支持，并相信万智牌社区会对这一公告充满热情。我们希望领导层能够选择真诚地参与我们的努力并自愿承认我们的工会。从那时起，我们将开始集体谈判，为 Arena 背后的人员争取更好的待遇和工作条件。这对 Arena 团队来说是一个重要的时刻，我们很高兴能与大家一起踏上这段旅程。我们的要求旨在维护我们作为工人的权利和福祉。这不仅是 Arena 团队向前迈出的一步，也是整个游戏行业向前迈出的一步。我们正在证明，一支非凡的游戏制作团队可以齐心协力，创造出真正神奇的东西。我们正在建设我们希望在世界上看到的游戏产业，我们希望您能与我们合作。

Original Summary

  1. Today is an exciting day in the history of Magic: The Gathering Arena! We, the workers of the Arena team have come together to unionize as United Wizards of the Coast - CWA. Last week, a supermajority of eligible Arena workers signed union cards to pledge their support. Earlier this morning, we informed WOTC leadership of our intent to unionize, and called on the company to voluntarily recognize our union: https://unitedwizardsofthecoast.com/letterSo, what’s next? We’ve shown that we have overwhelming support from the Arena team and believe that the Magic community will be enthusiastic about this announcement. We hope that leadership will choose to engage with our efforts in good faith and voluntarily recognize our union. From there, we’ll begin our collective bargaining for better treatment and working conditions for the people behind Arena.This is a massive moment for the Arena team, and we’re thrilled to embark on this journey with you all. Our demands aim to preserve our rights and wellbeing as workers. This is a step forward not just for the Arena team, but the gaming industry as a whole. We’re demonstrating that a phenomenal team of game makers can come together to create something truly magical.We are building the game industry we want to see in the world, and we hope you will work with us.

bwestergard 2026/04/28 03:57
我很高兴看到这一点。

我不从事游戏工作，但我是一名软件开发人员，也是美国通信工人协会的成员。我还请了假来帮助游戏工作人员进行组织。

I'm glad to see this.

I don't work in games, but I am a software developer and a member of the Communications Workers of America. I've also taken leave to help workers in games organize.

I'm seeing a lot of ideological takes that are disconnected from the reality of unions with software developer members today. If anyone has questions about the CWA or game worker organizing campaigns, I'll do my best to answer.

northern-lights 2026/04/28 05:21
这个线程充满了如此多的反工会言论，你不得不怀疑它们是否是付费机器人。

如果您假设访问该网站的大多数人群都在技术或技术相关领域工作，那么您如何能反对一个主要目标是维护您的利益并为您的雇主利益而工作的实体呢？令人难以理解的是，人们竟然愿意做这么多违背自己最大利益的事情。或者……他们是机器人。

This thread is filled with so many anti-union takes that you have to wonder if they are paid bots.

If you assume that most of the crowd that visits this website works in tech or tech-adjacent fields, how can you be against an entity whose main objective is to safeguard and work for your interests over your employers? Unfathomable that people are willing to do so much against their own best interests. Or...they are bots.

在 HN 中查看
scoofy 2026/04/28 06:58
工会很复杂。一般来说，它们对工人有利，但当他们开始关注工作保障而不是薪酬并建立基于资历的优势时，工会的影响力就可以用来对付消费者和同事，而不仅仅是针对雇主。

“工会绝对是好的”与“工会绝对是坏的”一样天真。工会如何优先考虑其权力始终是一个问题，从长远来看，这可能会导致不良做法。

如果你总体上关心公平，而不仅仅是“什么对我个人有利”，你就不必费力去寻找强大的工会的恶意行为。

Unions are complicated. Generally speaking they are good for workers, but when they start focusing on job security over compensation and build in seniority-based advantages, the leverage unions have can be wielded against consumers and fellow workers, not just against employers.

“Unions are unequivocally good” is about as naive as “unions are unequivocally bad.” It’s always a question of how the union prioritizes their power, and that can lead to bad practices in the long run.

If you care about fairness, generally, and not just “what’s good for me personally” you don’t have to look hard to see powerful unions acting in bad faith.

在 HN 中查看
bb88 2026/04/28 08:19
另一方面，列出恶意行为和虐待员工的公司名单比查找工会的虐待行为要庞大得多。

因行为恶劣而解雇经理只会为受害者提供法律依据。 这也是为什么城市不会解雇坏警察，而是留住他们直到悬而未决的诉讼得到解决。

On the other hand, it's far more voluminous to catalog the list of corporations acting in bad faith and abusing their employees than finding the abuses of unions.

Firing managers for egregious behavior only makes the legal case for the victims. That's also why cities don't fire bad cops, but instead keep them around until pending litigation is resolved.

在 HN 中查看
scoofy 2026/04/28 08:42
如果“谁更糟糕”是一个相关指标，那么工会问题就不会那么复杂。不过，这又是对非常复杂的现实的一种完全天真的看法。

企业“更糟糕”的一个非常明显的原因就是它们拥有更多的杠杆作用。 “杠杆可能被滥用”的想法对于该范式来说是一个更加深思熟虑的启发。

If “who is worse” is a relevant metric, the question of unions would not be complex. Again, though, this is an entirely naive view of what is a very complicated reality.

The very obvious reason that corporations are “worse” is simply that they have more leverage. The idea that “leverage is likely to be abused” is a much more thoughtful heuristic for the paradigm.

在 HN 中查看
bb88 2026/04/28 09:38
你的观点已被注意到。

但工会从来都不是凭空存在的。 如果不了解它们产生的原因，即公司虐待员工，那么很容易说“工会很复杂”，因为它们存在的世界比工会复杂得多，甚至可能更加邪恶。

Your point is noted.

But unions have never existed in a vacuum. And without the context of why they came about, that is from corporations abusing employees, it's easy to say "Unions are complex" when the world in which they exist is far more complex than unions are and perhaps far more vile.

在 HN 中查看
scoofy 2026/04/28 09:59
我一般是支持工会的。不要仅仅因为我批评了他们就认为我认为他们通常不是一个好主意。我所指出的问题是民主的普遍问题。当谈到当代人的谈判能力时，现任者往往会忽视后代人的福祉。

我想回应的一点是：“这条线索充满了如此多的反工会言论，你不得不怀疑它们是否是付费机器人。”

我认为普通人反对工会的原因有很多，一般来说，这是因为不同的工人群体处于不同的位置，有不同的观点。

一般来说，如果有某种“工人权利法案”纳入有组织的劳动法中，以防止这些虐待行为，就会有 很多 总体上对工会提供更强有力的支持。

你想成为一名码头工人吗？太糟糕了，作为码头工人，这些工作不适合你……而且这是一个 完全巧合 码头工人的极高薪工作往往属于现有码头工人的孩子。更不用说他们努力阻止目前在大多数其他国家已成为标准的技术改进。

您希望医疗费用下降吗？糟糕的是，医学专业组织已经设法人为地限制医学院学生和住院医师的数量。

如果工会的活动受到限制 扼杀自己行业内的竞争，如果对表现不佳的工会成员的工作保障程度进行限制，如果有合法的规则规定精英管理必须成为规则而不是例外，那么我认为绝大多数美国人会开始呼吁增加工会成员资格。目前我们看到的是 很多好的工作，而且还有许多封地被建立和锁定。

有工会保护工人免受公司的虐待行为。还有一些工会保护点灯工的工作免受电灯泡的“暴政”，并在这个过程中让每个人都变得更穷。

I'm generally pro-union. Don't think that just because I criticize them that I don't think they're generally a good idea. The problems I'm pointing to are general problems of democracy in general. Incumbents tend to ignore future generations well-beings when it comes to current generations ability to negotiate.

The point I'm trying to respond to is: "This thread is filled with so many anti-union takes that you have to wonder if they are paid bots."

I think there are plenty of reasons why normal folks are anti-union, and generally, it's because different sets of workers are in different positions and have different perspectives.

Generally speaking, if there were some kind of "Workers Bill of Rights" built into organized labor law, preventing these abuses, there would be much stronger support for unions generally.

You want to be a longshoreman? Tough shit, they aren't any jobs for you as a longshoreman... and it is a total coincidence that the extremely high paying gigs for longshoremen tend go to the children of existing longshoremen. Not to mention their effort to shut out technological improvements that are standard in most other countries now.

You want medical costs to go down? Tough shit, the professional organizations for medicine have managed to artificially limit the number of med school students and residencies.

If there were limits on what unions could to stifle competition within their own industries, if there were limits on the extent of job security for poorly performing union members, if there were legitimate rules that meritocracy has to be the rule, not the exception, then I think the vast majority of Americans would start clamoring for more union membership. What we currently see is a lot of good work, but also a lot of fiefdoms being established and locked down.

There are unions that protect workers from firm's abusive practices. There are also unions that protect lamplighters job from the "tyranny" of the electric light bulb, and make everyone poorer in the process.

wredcoll 2026/04/28 10:49
> 公司“更糟糕”的一个非常明显的原因就是它们拥有更多的杠杆作用。 “杠杆可能被滥用”的想法对于该范式来说是一个更加深思熟虑的启发。

我想，如果你喜欢终结陈词滥调的思想。

公司、 一般来说，有一套非常不同的激励措施和他们运用权力的方式以及人们的方式 外部 他们的权力结构可以与他们互动。

当人们试图声称有权做某事的公司与拥有相同权力的民主政府相同时，这也是同样的问题。它不是。

> The very obvious reason that corporations are “worse” is simply that they have more leverage. The idea that “leverage is likely to be abused” is a much more thoughtful heuristic for the paradigm.

If you enjoy thought terminating clichés, I suppose.

Corporations, in general, have a very different set incentives and ways they can wield power and ways that people outside of their power structures can interact with them.

It's the same issue when people try to claim a corporation having the power to do X is the same as a democratic government having the same power. It's not.

aidenn0 2026/04/28 07:04
> ...您如何能反对一个主要目标是维护您的利益并为您的雇主利益而努力的实体？

我个人倾向于支持工会，但只需很少的同情心就能理解反对工会的人不相信工会会服务于他们既定的目的。

> ...how can you be against an entity whose main objective is to safeguard and work for your interests over your employers?

This is like asking how people can be anti Google when Google's mission is to "organize the world's information and make it universally accessible and useful."

I personally lean pro-union, but it takes very little empathy to understand that the people who are anti-union don't believe that the unions will serve their stated purpose.

xboxnolifes 2026/04/28 05:40
我不认为它们是机器人，因为我很长时间以来一直在 HN 上看到这种情绪。相反，它让我想起人们将自己视为“暂时尴尬的百万富翁”的想法。如果你认为自己未来是资本阶级的一部分，并认为强大的工会会伤害资本阶级，那么人们可能会反对他们。

Imnimo 2026/04/28 03:42
当《Arena》首次宣布时，他们的文章中有一句有趣的台词：

https://magic.wizards.com/en/news/feature/everything-you-nee...

>我们创建了一个全新的游戏规则引擎（GRE），它使用复杂的机器学习，可以读取我们可以为万智牌梦想的任何纸牌。这意味着我们行业领先的设计师不再受束缚，可以围绕新机制和意想不到但非常有趣的概念来构建和创造卡牌和深入的游戏玩法，所有这些都可以通过新的 GRE 来适应 MTG Arena。

当时，这种使用“复杂的机器学习”（显然？）将自然语言卡片文本翻译成规则引擎可以执行的代码的说法让我觉得明显是假的。近十年后的今天，人工智能开始达到一个合理的水平。

工会在信中写道：

>在过去的几年里，领导层施加的压力越来越大，要求在 WOTC 工作的各个方面采用法学硕士和 Gen AI 工具，这通常是出于受影响员工的明确担忧

我很好奇，随着技术的成熟，这是否会包括反对将 WotC 的古老幻想变成现实？

Back when Arena was first announced, there was an interesting line in their write-up:

https://magic.wizards.com/en/news/feature/everything-you-nee...

>We've created an all-new Games Rules Engine (GRE) that uses sophisticated machine learning that can read any card we can dream up for Magic. That means the shackles are off for our industry-leading designers to build and create cards and in-depth gameplay around new mechanics and unexpected but widly fun concepts, all of which can be adapted for MTG Arena thanks to the new GRE under the hood.

At the time, this claim of using "sophisticated machine learning" to (apparently?) translate natural language card text into code that a rules engine could enforce struck me as obviously fake. Now nearly ten years later, AI is starting to reach a level where this is plausible.

In their letter, the union writes:

>Over the past few years, pressure has ramped up from leadership to adopt LLMs and Gen AI tools in various aspects of our work at WOTC, often over the explicit concerns of impacted employees

I'm curious if this would include fighting against turning WotC's old fanciful claim into a reality as the technology matures?

在 HN 中查看
JRandomHacker42 2026/04/28 04:24
Arena 卡引擎基于 CLIPS [1]，而不是基于现代 LLM 的工具。魔法卡是用一种非常受限制的语言（通常称为“卡模板”）编写的，这种语言非常适合机器解析。

[1]： https://www.clipsrules.net/

The Arena card engine is based on CLIPS [1] and not modern LLM-based tools. Magic cards are written in a very constrained language (usually called "card templating") that lends itself very well to machine-parseability.

[1]: https://www.clipsrules.net/

在 HN 中查看
tanjtanjtanj 2026/04/28 05:07
在大约 10 到 15 年的时间里，情况确实如此，但现代卡牌经常依赖于非常宽松的语言，它们在发布之前或有时在发布之后会通过规则收紧。请参阅最新集合中“Prepared”关键字背后的语言，了解一个引人注目的示例。
There was an era about 10-15 years long where that was true but modern cards often fall back on very loose language that they tighten up with rulings prior or sometimes after release. See the language behind the "Prepared" key word in the newest set for a striking example.
在 HN 中查看
JRandomHacker42 2026/04/28 05:22
我认为Prepared一点也不含糊。它的含义在CR（722）中定义，并且每张使用它的卡都有明确的触发条件或“进入准备”替换效果。这只是一个新名称，而且已经有很多这样的名称，包括已有 10 年以上历史的名称（著名、怪物、升级）。
I don't think Prepared is ambiguous at all. It has its meaning defined in the CR (722) and every card that uses it has either a clear trigger condition or the "enters prepared" replacement effect. It's just a new designation and there are plenty of those already, including ones that are 10+ years old (Renowned, Monstrous, Level Up).
在 HN 中查看
tanjtanjtanj 2026/04/28 05:49
法官们对这意味着什么进行了广泛的讨论。简单阅读一下，它并没有按照它所说的那样做。从短语“它的咒语”开始——这些词在进入裁决之前完全没有定义，并且并不意味着它们在其他上下文中的含义。它也没有提及之前的规则，它也有点破坏了这一点。

我不知道规则 722 与准备有什么关系。

Well there was a huge discussion on what it means amongst judges. On plain reading it does not do what it states it does. Start just with the phrase "its spell" - those words are entirely undefined until getting into the rulings, and don't mean what they mean in other contexts. It makes no mention of the prior rules that it sort of hacks into this either.

I have no idea what rule 722 has to do with prepared.

在 HN 中查看
JRandomHacker42 2026/04/28 05:59
我是一名法官，几乎没有看到任何有关“Prepared”的讨论（大部分只是围绕与的交互进行澄清。

规则722是“准备卡”的规则，所以我不明白它怎么可能不相关。

文本“itspell”仅出现在提醒文本中，该文本不是规则文本，不会包含在模板语言中。

I'm a judge and have seen barely any discussion around Prepared (mostly just clarification around the interaction with.

Rule 722 is the rule for "Preparation Cards", so I fail to see how it could not be relevant.

The text "its spell" only occur in reminder text, which is not rules text and would not be included in template language.

在 HN 中查看
tanjtanjtanj 2026/04/28 06:10
啊，我猜我有一个旧版本的 CR，是我当法官时下载的，其中规则第 722 节是“控制另一个玩家”，那里有奇怪的重组。

您是否活跃于法官论坛或社交媒体？大量的线索已经准备好这些论点（我没有提出这个想法，因为我现在不再玩或判断）。

不管你是否认为这个例子令人困惑，WOTC 几年前就曾表示，他们正在牺牲清晰度以换取更自然的语言作为开发目标，这一点在卡片上显而易见。

抱歉，扔掉一条文字是因为它的提醒文字是一种逃避，这是 99% 以上的玩家与规则互动的唯一方式。该规则在演示时是有意义的，但从逻辑上一步步遵循实际卡片上的内容来看，它实际上并没有以符合其应该工作方式的方式发挥作用。

Ah, I have I guess an old version of the CR downloaded from when I was a judge and TO where rule section 722 is "Controlling Another Player," weird reorganization there.

Are you active in judge forums or social media at all? Huge threads on prepared with these arguments (I didn't come up with the idea as I now longer play or judge).

Regardless of whether you think this one example is confusing, WOTC came out a few years back and said they were sacrificing clarity for more natural language as a development goal and it's clearly noticeable in the cards.

Sorry, throwing out a text because its reminder text is a cop-out, that's the only way 99%+ of players are going to interact with the rules. The rule makes sense when demonstrated but from a logical step-by-step when following what it says on the actual cards it does not actually function in a way that conforms to the way it is supposed to work.

在 HN 中查看
JRandomHacker42 2026/04/28 06:23
我特别谈论“我们可以使用自然语言工具来解析预言文本并在竞技场中生成功能游戏对象”的用例。对于该用例，查看实际规则文本而不是提醒文本是完全明智的。

进一步回想起来，在预览季期间，当人们看到“假的/泄露的”模型上有不正确的文本时，人们会感到困惑，但这对于“WotC 自己编写可以解析卡片文本的系统”的问题来说也不是问题。

I'm specifically talking about the use case of "can we use natural-language tools to parse oracle text and produce functioning game objects in Arena". For that use case, it's completely sensible to look at the actual rules text and not reminder text.

Looking back further, there was confusion during preview season when people were looking at "fake/leaked" mockups that had incorrect text on them, but this also isn't a problem for the issue of "WotC themselves writing systems that can parse card text".

在 HN 中查看
cleversomething 2026/04/28 05:53
我认为使用前面讨论过的 CLIPS 系统可以很容易地加强模糊程度。它不是防错的，之前需要手动调整，但它比我们现在认为的由法学硕士支持的工具的人工智能更加“坚固”。
I think that level of ambiguity would be fairly easy to tighten up using the CLIPS system that was previously discussed. It isn't bug-proof and has needed manual tune-ups before but it's much more "hardened" than what we think of as AI now with LLM-powered tools.
在 HN 中查看
nicolas-siplis 2026/04/28 03:55
我现在实际上正在研究这个！ https://chiplis.com/ironsmith

它是一个解析器+（反）编译器和规则引擎，我试图对所有标准/现代/复古/指挥官合法卡牌实现 100% 的覆盖。其中大约 23000 个得到部分支持，而目前有 15k 个完全支持（比 MTGA 当前支持的 IIRC 多约 3k）。它还允许 P2P 4 路多人游戏，遗憾的是 Arena 不允许：/

I'm actually working on this right now! https://chiplis.com/ironsmith

It's a parser + (de)compiler and rules engine which I'm trying to get to 100% coverage over all Standard/Modern/Vintage/Commander legal cards. About 23000 of them are partially supported, while 15k currently work in full (~3k more than what MTGA currently supports, IIRC). It also allows for P2P 4-way multiplayer which Arena unfortunately does not :/

cleversomething 2026/04/28 05:50

我认为这封信中的措辞是指使用法学硕士来生成图像和音乐等创意资产。

As others have said, there is an actual concrete system for translating card text into rules, and it's not an LLM (which would be a disaster).

I assume the wording in this letter is referring to using LLMs to generate slop as creative assets like images and music.

在 HN 中查看
nonethewiser 2026/04/28 03:49
我理解为什么视频游戏行业的工人想要成立工会。他们喜欢这个行业，但标准很糟糕。我不知道工会将如何在视频游戏行业流行起来。

成立工会的能力与劳动力的意识形态关系不大，而与行业结构密切相关。

当劳动力不可替代、工作场所规模庞大且集中、如果工会能够停止其行业之外的关键业务（即铁路、港口等）、竞争/离岸外包等风险较低等情况下，工会往往会流行起来。视频游戏行业本身对于工会化来说还不太成熟。

I understand why workers in the video game industry want to unionize. They like the industry but the standards are shit. I do not see how unions will catch on in the video game industry.

The ability to unionize has very little to do with the ideology of the workforce and everything to do with the structure of the industry.

Unions tend to catch on when labor is irreplacable, workplace is large and centralized, if they can halt critical operations beyond their industry (ie railroads, ports, etc), there is low exposure to competition/off shoring, etc. The video game industry itself is not very ripe for unionizatoin.

在 HN 中查看
rhcom2 2026/04/28 03:54

I'm not sure that's true. Surely doormen, janitors, and security guards are not "irreplaceable" and can't "halt critical operations beyond their industry".
在 HN 中查看
xienze 2026/04/28 04:57
这些都是只能在“此时此地”完成的劳动。不能只让印度人通过互联网来做这件事。由于罢工工人骚扰和攻击替代者，临时更换工人通常会变得复杂。同样，当你的替代者在地球的另一边时，很难做到这一点。
That's all labor that can only be done "here and now." Can't just have Indians do it over the internet. And replacing the workers at a moment's notice is usually complicated by the striking workers harassing and attacking the replacements. Again, hard to do that when your replacement is on the other side of the globe.

xienze 2026/04/28 06:50

如果你将所有的知识输入法学硕士，并在所有艺术品上训练扩散模型，我敢打赌你可以让火车从印度一直运行到世界末日。唯一会让它脱轨的是，如果顾客_知道_它不是“正品”。

> You cannot simply transfer Magic the Gathering to overseas workers and expect any kind of usable result.

如果游戏开发商的每个人都罢工，基本上没有多少外包或工贼可以替代他们。这实际上可能适用于基本上所有软件

If everyone at a game developer goes on strike, there is basically no amount of outsourcing or scab labour that can replace them. This is actually probably true of basically all software

在 HN 中查看
michaelt 2026/04/28 04:37
一些 历史上实力雄厚的工会之所以享有权力，是因为他们的罢工不仅使雇主无法赚钱，而且给下游的许多人带来了极大的不便。

如果卡车司机或码头工人罢工，货架上就会没有食物；如果煤矿工人罢工，灯就会熄灭，等等。

因此，雇主达成交易的动机不仅仅是因为错失了赚钱的机会，还因为政治家、其他强大的资本家和公众舆论。

当然，有很多工会的情况并非如此。剧院和好莱坞都成立了工会，尽管事实上没有人在罢工时被冻住或挨饿。

我认为，游戏开发者比码头工人的职位更接近好莱坞的职位。

Some historically powerful unions have enjoyed their power because their strikes not only stop their employers making money, but also impose great inconvenience on many people downstream of them.

If truckers or dockworkers go on strike there's no food on the shelves, if coal miners go on strike the lights go out, and so on.

As a consequence of this, employers are motivated to make a deal not just by missed opportunities to make money, but also by politicians, other powerful capitalists, and public opinion.

Of course there are plenty of unions where this isn't the case; theatres and hollywood are unionised despite the fact nobody freezes or starves when they go on strike.

Game developers are, I think, closer to the hollywood position than the dockworkers position.

Well, the theater and Hollywood workers themselves starve, as seen in the last recent strike, with many not being able to make ends meet.

iwhalen 2026/04/28 03:07
信件全文[1]：

> 我们的空闲时间是我们自己的：目前，如果员工在空闲时间利用自己的资源做出任何创意，孩之宝可能会声称拥有所有权。我们在空闲时间做什么不应该由公司决定；我们在空闲时间所做的事情也不应该归公司所有。

这在创意领域有多常见？

从我的角度来看，这似乎很奇怪。想象一下，在您的个人计算机上进行自由开源软件工作或业余项目，而您的公司试图获得它。奇怪的...

[1]： https://unitedwizardsofthecoast.com/letter

From the full letter[1]:

> Our Free Time is Our Own: Currently, if an employee makes anything creative in their free time, with their own resources, Hasbro may claim ownership. What we do in our free time should not be dictated by the company; neither should what we make in our free time be owned by the company.

How common is this in creative fields?

From my perspective this seems outlandish. Imagine doing FOSS work or a side project on your personal computer and your company tries to claim it. Odd...

[1]: https://unitedwizardsofthecoast.com/letter

在 HN 中查看
drob518 2026/04/28 03:16
大多数科技公司的雇佣协议都有一项条款，规定雇主拥有你在为他们工作期间所做的一切。如果你拿薪水，而不是按小时工作，那就真的没有“空闲时间”。实际上，没有公司会因为任何非竞争性的事情而追究你的责任，如果它也是开源工作，那就更是如此。但是，是的，如果你在“业余”时间发明有竞争力的产品，公司可能会追随你。
Most employment agreements for tech companies have a clause that says that the employer owns everything you do while you’re working for them. And if you’re on salary, as opposed to working by the hour, there really is no “free time.” In practice no company is going to go after you for anything that is non-competitive, and doubly so if it’s also open source work. But yea, if you’re inventing competitive products in your “spare” time, companies could go after you.
在 HN 中查看
margalabargala 2026/04/28 03:47
最多？不，当然有很多。

> 有一项条款规定雇主拥有您在为他们工作期间所做的一切

好的公司会有一个条款，说雇主拥有你所做的一切 在公司相关领域 在为他们工作时，明确命名该领域。

如果您在物流公司工作，您将可以毫无顾虑地编写自己的视频编辑器。如果你在一家不烂的公司工作。

Most? No. Many, sure.

> have a clause that says that the employer owns everything you do while you’re working for them

Good companies will have a clause that says the employer owns everything you do in the relevant field of the company while working for them, explicitly naming that field.

If you work for a logistics company, you would be able to write your own video editor without any worry. If you work for a not-shitty company.

在 HN 中查看
drob518 2026/04/28 04:15
我曾在硅谷的 12 家公司工作过，他们都有这种语言的变体，无论是财富 50 强的大型公司，还是公司注册文件上墨迹未干的 4 人初创公司。是的，更好的人有一个条款限制对竞争性工作的要求，但并非所有人都这样做。小公司对你的态度会更灵活，特别是如果你是关键员工的话。在大公司，通常是要么接受要么放弃。人力资源部门有必须签署的表格，而且他们肯定不会让法律部门为您介入。需要明确的是，没有人会追随你以获得完全正交的工作产品。这是没有意义的，因为他们无法将你创建的任何正交事物货币化，而且这会让他们付出没有回报的律师费。但我以前见过一些公司试图执行这些合同。我还没有见过有人上法庭。他们通常会在事情进展到这一步之前就达成和解，但有传言称知识产权已被授予该公司。不管怎样，这名员工还是离开了，并在外面重新做了工作。
I’ve worked at 12 companies in Silicon Valley and they all had variations of such language, whether huge Fortune 50 corporations or 4-person startups with the ink still wet on their incorporation filing. Yes, the better ones have a clause that restricts the claim to competitive work, but not all do. Smaller companies will be more flexible with you, particularly if you’re a key hire. At larger companies, it’s typically take it or leave it. HR has forms that must be signed and they sure aren’t going to involve the legal department for you. And to be clear, nobody is going to come after you for a completely orthogonal work product. There’s no point because they can’t monetize whatever orthogonal thing you created and it would cost them lawyers fees for no return. But I have seen companies try to enforce those contracts before. I have not ever seen one go to court. They typically settle before they get that far, but rumors were that IP was given to the company. The employee left in any case and redid the work outside.
在 HN 中查看
margalabargala 2026/04/28 04:27
> 是的，更好的人有一个条款限制对竞争性工作的要求，但并非所有人都这样做。

是的，这就是我的全部观点。你的其余评论，我同意。在实践中，你不太可能对正交的东西，尤其是在经济上毫无价值的东西产生问题。

但你可能会。有很多人对知识产权抱有掠夺性态度，特别是在高管或未来的高管中。

限制竞争知识产权的公司确实存在。

> Yes, the better ones have a clause that restricts the claim to competitive work, but not all do.

Yeah, this is my whole point. The rest of your comment, I agree with. In practice you're unlikely to have an issue for something orthogonal and especially something financially worthless.

But you might. There are a lot of people out there that have predatory attitudes towards IP, especially among C-level or would-be-C-level.

And companies that restrict to competing IP do exist.

在 HN 中查看
drob518 2026/04/28 04:40
当然，它已经转向限制竞争知识产权的范围，但这只是在过去 15 年左右的时间里。在此之前，它绝对是包罗万象的。但是，是的，随着关键员工的抵制，公司变得更加开明，而且公司发现他们并不真正想要你的正交想法。但在当时，尤其是当许多员工最多只能为三到五家公司工作时，“我们拥有您创造的一切”条款是非常常见的。
It’s certainly shifted toward restricting the scope to competing IP, but that’s only in the last 15 years or so. Prior to that it was definitely more all encompassing. But yea, companies are becoming more enlightened as key employees push back and the companies figure out that they don’t really want your orthogonal ideas anyway. But back in the day, particularly when many employees might only work for a maximum of three to five companies anyway, it was extremely common to have “we own everything you create” clauses.

junon 2026/04/28 03:34

在 HN 中查看
kimixa 2026/04/28 03:55
这也是我试图要求的——通常我从招聘经理和人力资源部门得到“没关系”，但两次我都不得不推迟并将其添加到实际提供的合同中。那是非常多的 不是 简单的。

即使这样，这些个人项目通常也会有“足够不同的部门”的要求——这是有道理的，但它的措辞不可避免地足够模糊，以至于几乎任何项目都可能需要上法庭来证明它是 不是 直接相关。如果这种关系真的变成敌对的，那么对于我个人来说，这将是极其昂贵的。

This is also something I try to ask for - generally I get the "that's fine" from the hiring manager and HR, but both times I've then had to push back and get it added to the actual supplied contract. And that was very much not easy.

And even then there's normally a "Sufficiently Different Sector" requirement for those personal projects - which makes sense, but it is inevitably worded vague enough that it would likely require going to court for pretty much any project to show it's not directly related. And that would be near prohibitively expensive for me as an individual if the relationship actually became adversarial.

在 HN 中查看
drob518 2026/04/28 03:48
可以，但是你必须向公司申报，公司必须批准。如果他们不这样做，因为竞争激烈，那么你就不走运了。
Yes, but you have to declare them to the company and the company must approve them. If they don’t, because it’s competitive, you’re out of luck.
在 HN 中查看
deanputney 2026/04/28 03:42
我认为这是一种必须明确记录的例外情况或例外情况。问题是文档的呈现并不那么简单。
I’ve seen this done as a carve-out or exception that has to be explicitly documented. Trouble is that documentation is not presented as simple.

paulddraper 2026/04/28 03:23
相关性并不完全是工作时间，而是“受雇工作”，即如果它在您的有偿责任范围内。

包括加利福尼亚州在内的少数州不允许这种条件。

The relevance isn’t wholly work hours but rather “work for hire,” I.e. if it’s in the scope of your paid responsibilities.

A handful of states including California disallow this condition.

vidarh 2026/04/28 03:11
即使在科技领域也很常见，我的合同都试图要求这一点，并且合同明确排除了将其作为公司有多伟大的证据。
Common enough even in tech that I've both had contracts try to demand this, and had contracts explicitly rule it out being presented as evidence of how great the company was.
在 HN 中查看
stego-tech 2026/04/28 03:16
借调了。我必须是 非常 小心翼翼地从事与我的主要工作完全脱节的副业项目， 和 必须首先获得法律批准。

这 共同态度 公司的一个特点是，他们为你在“工作”内外的一生付出代价，而这些工会就是对这种侵犯（以及一般相关的薪酬不足）的回应。

对他们很好。祝您谈判公平合同好运！

Seconded. I had to be very careful to work on side projects completely divorced from my main job for a spell, and had to get legal approval first.

The common attitude of companies is that they’re paying for the whole of your life inside and outside of “work”, and these Unions are a response to that encroachment (and associated under-compensation in general).

Good on them. Best of luck negotiating a fair contract!

在 HN 中查看
DelaneyM 2026/04/28 03:20
这一点也不罕见，而且很重要。

当某人被授权远程工作、领取薪水且不受特定工作时间限制时，就很难以法律上一致的方式确定哪些工作是“他们的”、哪些工作是“公司的”。 是的，从上下文来看，这通常是显而易见的，但上下文并不总是适用于法庭。 它可能特别混乱，因为一个人贡献的开源项目类型经常与他们日常工作重叠。

因此，大多数受薪并允许在家办公的公司通常会要求员工明确列出他们所从事的任何他们不希望公司拥有的项目，并期望未列出的所有项目都归公司所有。 这有点麻烦，但通常是最不坏的选择。

在我们公司，如果我们在开放源码软件或宠物项目上工作，我们需要提交一份表格，据我所知，没有人的申请被拒绝过。

编辑：这很重要，因为它是对称的 - 这不仅定义了公司的财产不是什么，还定义了公司的财产是什么。 因此，如果您出于公司目的提出一个巧妙的问题解决方案并将其引入 OSS 项目，那么它就不会再困扰公司。

It's not at all uncommon, and important.

When someone is empowered to work remotely, and is salaried and not held to specific hours, then it's very hard to identify what work is "theirs" and what work is "the company's" in a legally consistent way. Yes, it's usually obvious from context, but context doesn't always carry to a court of law. It can be particularly messy because the kinds of open source projects one contributes to often overlap with the work they do in their day job.

So most companies which are salaried and allow WFH will usually ask employees to explicitly list any project they work on which they don't want owned by the company, with the expectation being that everything unlisted is owned by the company. It's a bit cumbersome, but generally the least bad option.

At our company we have a form to file if we do work outside of hours on OSS or pet projects, and to the best of my knowledge nobody has ever had their application denied.

edit: it's important because it's symmetric - not only does this define what _isn't_ property of the company, it defines what _is_. So if you come up with a clever solution to a problem for a company purpose and introduce it into an OSS project, it doesn't come back to haunt the company.

在 HN 中查看
sophacles 2026/04/28 03:38
另外值得注意的是——公司可能不介意你做这项工作 今天 但如果没有任何书面形式，公司将来可能会来找你。当您领取薪水并为一家可能被收购或经历重大董事会更替的公司工作时，这一点尤其重要。我有几个非常支持项目和支持开源软件的雇主明确表示，他们会批准任何不与核心业务竞争的东西，但为了在收购后的理想未来中保护我自己，他们会以书面形式将其批准。
Also worth noting - the company may not mind you doing that work today but without anything in writing the company may come after you in the future. This is particularly relevant when you're on salary and work for a company that may be acquired or experience significant board turnover. I've had several employers who were very pro- side project and pro-OSS explicitly state that they'll approve anything that doesn't compete with the core business, but get it in writing for my own protection in ideal future of post-acquisition.
在 HN 中查看
bluefirebrand 2026/04/28 05:03
> 当某人被授权远程工作、领取薪水且不受特定工作时间限制时，就很难以法律上一致的方式确定哪些工作是“他们的”、哪些工作是“公司的”

这很容易。在公司发行的硬件上完成的工作属于公司。其他一切都没有

> When someone is empowered to work remotely, and is salaried and not held to specific hours, then it's very hard to identify what work is "theirs" and what work is "the company's" in a legally consistent way

It's easy. Work done on company issued hardware belongs to the company. Everything else does not

在 HN 中查看
margalabargala 2026/04/28 03:40
不，实际上只有那些不在乎自己很糟糕的公司才会这么做。正是对员工的无情缺乏尊重才导致了你所描述的情况，尽管你说得对，这种情况并不少见。

任何一家还算不错的公司都会在合同中限制与公司业务领域相关的知识产权。如果你写物流软件，公司会说“我们拥有你写的所有物流软件”。你无法创造一个竞争对手。但是，如果您在一家物流软件公司工作并决定利用自己的时间编写视频编辑器，那么该公司将不会拥有它。

No, it's really only companies that don't care about being shitty that do this. It's a callous lack of regard for employees that leads to the situation you describe, though you're right that it's not uncommon.

Any halfway decent company will restrict in the contract to IP that's related to the company's area of business. If you write logistics software, the company will say "we own all logistics software you write". You can't create a competitor. But if you work for a logistics software company and decide to go write a video editor on your own time, the company wouldn't own that.

在 HN 中查看
mjr00 2026/04/28 03:57
> 如果你编写物流软件，公司会说“我们拥有你编写的所有物流软件”。你无法创造一个竞争对手。但是，如果您在一家物流软件公司工作并决定利用自己的时间编写视频编辑器，那么该公司将不会拥有它。

问题是“物流软件”的定义并没有明确的法律定义。视频编辑器是一个看似明显的例子，但如果你想出了一种新颖的优化技术，但它并不明显，该怎么办？ 一定 只适用于物流？你能将该软件分拆成一个单独的业务吗？更基本的东西怎么样，比如工具？想想像 Slack 这样的东西，它只是作为视频游戏开发的一部分而创建的内部消息传递工具。想象一下，在 Slack 起飞后，一名员工声称，由于他们至少部分是在工作时间之外编写的 Slack，而且它不是“视频游戏软件”，因此公司不拥有该软件的所有权。

这就是为什么常见的做法是公司拥有除明确划定的任何东西之外的一切，它避免了这样的含糊不清。

> If you write logistics software, the company will say "we own all logistics software you write". You can't create a competitor. But if you work for a logistics software company and decide to go write a video editor on your own time, the company wouldn't own that.

The problem is there's no clear legal definition of what "logistics software" is. A video editor is a seemingly obvious example of what is not, but what if you came up with a novel optimization technique which is not necessarily only applicable to logistics? Could you spin off that software into a separate business? What about something more fundamental, like tooling? Think about something like Slack, which was just meant to be an internal messaging tool created as part of the development of a video game. Imagine after Slack took off, that an employee claimed that because they had written Slack at least partly outside of working hours and it was not "video game software", the company didn't have ownership of the software.

This is why the common approach is that the company owns everything except anything explicitly carved out, it avoids ambiguities like this.

在 HN 中查看
margalabargala 2026/04/28 04:21
是的，你说得对。对于那些不在乎的公司来说，常见的（懒惰的）做法是“我们拥有一切，因为引入歧义可能会很复杂，但无论如何我们拥有你”。

对明显不相关的软件进行剥离很容易。在一家由讲道理的人组成的体面公司里，如果你想做一些你认为属于灰色地带的事情，你会提出要求并获得书面同意。这也是许多烂公司的运作方式！不同的是，那些烂公司 绝不 一条路线，可以做任何事情，无论多么不相关，而无需人力资源部门进行管理。因为他们认为他们拥有你。

> 想象一下，Slack 起飞后，一名员工声称，由于他们至少部分是在工作时间之外编写的 Slack，而且它不是“视频游戏软件”，因此公司不拥有该软件的所有权。

相反，使用非零的工作时间来完成某件事显然使其成为公司财产。

Yes, you're right. The common (lazy) approach for companies that don't give a shit is "we own everything because it might be complicated to introduce ambiguity and we own you anyway".

Having carveouts for obviously not-related software is easy. At a decent company populated by reasonable people, if you wanted to work on something you felt fell into the grey area, you would ask and get a written okay. Which is how it also works at many of the shitty companies! The difference is that the shitty companies there's never a route to do anything, however unrelated, without running it by HR. Because they think they own you.

> Imagine after Slack took off, that an employee claimed that because they had written Slack at least partly outside of working hours and it was not "video game software", the company didn't have ownership of the software.

On the contrary, using a nonzero amount of working hours to work on something pretty clearly makes it company property.

在 HN 中查看
mjr00 2026/04/28 04:46
> 不同之处在于，那些糟糕的公司从来没有一条路可以做任何事情，无论多么不相关，而不是由人力资源部门来运作。因为他们认为他们拥有你。

不过，“糟糕”的公司有法律义务包含此类条款，因为投资者和董事会成员不想处理“明显不相关的软件”存在这种模糊性的情况。人力资源的存在是为了保护公司。那些明显与你无关的事情可能不会被视为与董事会成员明显无关，因为董事会成员听说了你编写的新优化算法并意识到公司可以将其货币化。

> 相反，使用非零的工作时间来完成某件事显然使其成为公司财产。

您最近是否见过一份科技雇佣合同，其中规定您必须朝八晚五工作，并有 1 小时午休时间？灵活的工作时间意味着 任何 小时可能是“工作时间”。

> The difference is that the shitty companies there's never a route to do anything, however unrelated, without running it by HR. Because they think they own you.

"Shitty" companies have legal obligations to include these kind of clauses though, because investors and board members don't want to deal with a situation where there's exactly this ambiguity about what counts as "obviously not-related software". HR exists to protect the company. What counts as obviously non-related to you may not count as obviously non-related to a board member who hears about the new optimization algorithm you wrote and realizes the company can monetize it.

> On the contrary, using a nonzero amount of working hours to work on something pretty clearly makes it company property.

How recently have you seen a tech employment contract where you were obligated to work exactly 8 to 5 with a 1 hour lunch break? Flexible hours means any hour is potentially a "working hour".

在 HN 中查看
margalabargala 2026/04/28 05:32
>> 相反，使用非零的工作时间来完成某件事显然使其成为公司财产。

> 您最近是否见过一份科技雇佣合同，其中规定您必须朝八晚五工作，并有 1 小时午休时间？灵活的工作时间意味着任何时间都可能是“工作时间”。

不是我的意思。在 Slack 示例中，这是公司内部计划的工作，利用公司资源进行。开发公司内部使用的、公司有 Jira 票证的东西是相当公然的“公司时间”。

> 不过，“糟糕”的公司有法律义务包含此类条款，因为投资者和董事会成员不想处理这种情况

不想处理某事并不是不做某事的法律义务。我不想处理报税问题，但这并不意味着我现在有不报税的法律义务。这只是懒惰。

> > On the contrary, using a nonzero amount of working hours to work on something pretty clearly makes it company property.

> How recently have you seen a tech employment contract where you were obligated to work exactly 8 to 5 with a 1 hour lunch break? Flexible hours means any hour is potentially a "working hour".

Not what I meant. In the Slack example, it was planned work within the company, made using company resources. Working on something for internal company use that the company has a Jira ticket for is pretty blatantly on the side of "Company time".

> "Shitty" companies have legal obligations to include these kind of clauses though, because investors and board members don't want to deal with a situation

Not wanting to deal with something isn't a legal obligation not to do it. I don't want to deal with filing taxes but that doesn't mean I now have a legal obligation not to file taxes. It's just laziness.

在 HN 中查看
stego-tech 2026/04/28 03:21
不能代表创意领域，但它在科技领域非常普遍。 当工资意味着我们可以负担得起房租或可能的住房并且工作保障非常好时，这是可以忍受的，但情况已不再如此，因此人们开始抵制这种过度的过度扩张。

另请参阅“反兼职”和“反社交媒体”条款。 天哪，我以前见过人们因为约会资料而被解雇/纪律处分的奇怪故事。 如果政府不告诉他们“不”，公司就会竭尽全力。

Can't speak for creative fields, but it's remarkably common in tech. It was tolerable when wages meant we could afford rent or possibly a home and job security was excellent, but that's no longer the case, and thus folks are starting to push back on that excessive overreach.

See also "anti-moonlighting" and "anti-social media" clauses. Hell, I've seen the odd story of folks being fired/disciplined for their dating profiles before. If the government doesn't tell them no, companies will take every inch they can get.

jballanc 2026/04/28 03:17
完成本科学位后，我的第一份工作是对玉米淀粉进行质量分析。作为雇用条件，我必须签署一份文件，声明我发明的与玉米有关的任何东西都是我雇主的财产。
My first job after finishing my undergrad degree was performing quality analysis on corn starch. As a condition of employment, I had to sign a paper saying anything I invented related to corn was property of my employer.
在 HN 中查看
InitialLastName 2026/04/28 03:14
这在许多创意和技术领域极为常见。它通常仅限于与雇主领域和雇员职能相关的工作，但人们可以想象创意领域的一些雇主在他们的解释上会更广泛一些。
It's extremely common in lots of creative and technical fields. It is usually restricted to work related to the employer's field and the employee's function, but one could imagine some employers of folks in the creative arena being a bit more... expansive in their interpretation.
在 HN 中查看
littlecranky67 2026/04/28 03:21
这在德国很常见，至少在专利和发明范围内是这样。也就是说，如果您在工作之外创造任何发明并申请专利或将其推向市场，则您的雇主拥有该专利和利润（“Arbeitnehmererfindungen”）。幸运的是，缓慢的官僚政府有时会对我们有利，因为他们从不更新适用于软件的法律，而且软件在德国或欧盟不具有专利权 - 因此我们可以在软件方面开展副业项目，而不会影响我们。但如果你是机械工程师，那你就完蛋了。
This is common in Germany at least in the scope of patents and inventions. That is, if you make any invention at have it patented or market it outside of your job, your employer owns that patent and the profits ("Arbeitnehmererfindungen"). Luckily, a slow beaurocratic government works sometimes in our favour, as they never updates the law to apply to software, and software is not patentable in Germany or the EU - so we can work on side projects in software without that affecting us. But if you are a mechanical engineer, you are screwed.
在 HN 中查看
Dunedan 2026/04/28 03:34
这是不正确的。 “Arbeitnehmererfindungen”仅适用于您作为有偿工作的一部分所做的发明。看 https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arbeitnehmererfindung

无论你在业余时间做什么都取决于你，你的雇主对此没有发言权，除非他能证明这会对你的工作表现产生负面影响。

That's not correct. "Arbeitnehmererfindungen" only apply to inventions you make as part of your paid work. See https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arbeitnehmererfindung

Whatever you do in your spare time is up to you and your employer has no saying over it, unless he can prove that it negatively impacts your job performance.

thegrim33 2026/04/28 03:16

我觉得这是……合理的做法？如果你不同意他们的条件，那么..就不要在那里工作吗？

When I was looking for my last job a company wanted me to sign something similar to that. I declined their offer and got a job elsewhere instead.

I feel like that's .. the reasonable take here? If you don't agree to their conditions, then .. just don't work there?

在 HN 中查看
bee_rider 2026/04/28 03:29
基本上，关于就业的一切都是谈判，如果你不喜欢某个地方的协议，你可以尝试以不同的方式进行谈判，或者在其他地方工作。当然，成立工会是谈判中的合法举措。
It’s basically true that everything about employment is a negotiation and if you don’t like the deal at one place you can try to negotiate it differently, or work elsewhere. Of course, unionization is a legitimate move in that negotiation.

red-iron-pine 2026/04/28 03:48
我参与过的每一个 IT 和开发工作基本上都在说“你创建的任何东西都是我们的，我们对你找到的任何 LLC 或公司都有 ROFR”

在实践中，这要么是无法执行的，要么是对公司资金的巨大浪费，但如果从事工程或创意工作的人决定在其他地方窃取它，这是 CYA 的责任。

就像梅塔不会试图在农贸市场边的演出中偷走你当地的纸杯蛋糕

every IT and dev gig I've been at / around basically said "anything you create is ours and we have ROFR on any LLCs or companies you found"

in practice that is either unenforceable or else a giant waste of the company's money, but it's CYA in case someone doing engineering or creative work decides to rip it off elsewhere.

like Meta ain't gonna try to steal your local cupcakes at the farmer's market side gig

natbennett 2026/04/28 03:13
这样的条款在软件公司的雇佣合同中是相当标准的，至少在加州/硅谷地区是这样。有时，对于在您受雇之前明确命名的物品有例外，但有时他们也试图声称对您在加入公司之前制作的物品拥有所有权。
A clause like this is pretty standard in software company employment contracts, at least in the California/Silicon Valley zone. There’s sometimes an exception for explicitly named items that pre-date your employment but sometimes they try to claim ownership of stuff you made before joining the company too.

drob518 2026/04/28 03:18
通常，有一种方法可以在您进入公司之前声明您正在从事的工作，以防止他们试图起诉您以获得先前工作的权利。
Typically there’s a way to declare things that you are working on before you start at the company to prevent them trying to sue you for rights to prior work.

但这并不意味着它是可执行的。

Google has a similar clause in their employee contracts. I assume most tech companies do.

That doesn't mean it is enforceable, though.

在 HN 中查看
DaiPlusPlus 2026/04/28 03:48
与谷歌相反，我的前雇主微软可能拥有（拥有？）大型科技公司中最好的政策：兼职不仅被容忍，而且被积极鼓励！ （...当然，前提是它在 Windows 上运行）...因为如果这意味着接触新的 API/平台/库/概念，那么它基本上是免费的培训/经验 - 并且绝对有助于那些喜欢构建东西但最终在公司从事范围极其狭窄的工作的人的士气水平（例如，无法编写代码的 PM，或者只有在他们工作时才会受到管理层注意的 SDET 和 SRE） 不 做好他们的工作）。

在推出 Windows 8 期间，MSFT 的兼职政策也是他们的 Windows 应用商店战略的一部分：我们都被大力鼓励制作一个“Windows 应用商店应用程序”，这样 SteveB 就可以声称 MS 在其应用程序商店中拥有 N 个应用程序，因为领导层认为他们可以通过这种方式建立与苹果成熟的应用程序商店相比的可信度（当然，最终实际发生的是数百个 cr 应用程序，这些应用程序只是实时网站上的 WebView 包装）。

相比之下，我知道苹果可能有最糟糕的兼职政策：我被告知，除非你直接在 WebKit 或 Darwin 上工作，否则你必须停用你的 GitHub 帐户，否则就会发现自己很快就会被拖进众所周知的垃圾箱。

In contrast to Google, Microsoft, my former employer, probably has (had?) the best policy amongst big tech: moonlighting wasn't just tolerated, but actively encouraged! (...provided it runs on Windows, of course) ...because it's basically free training/experience if it means exposure to new APIs/platforms/libs/concepts - and definitely helps the morale levels of folks who love to build things but who ended-up with an extremely narrow-scoped job at the company (e.g. PMs who don't get to write code, or SDETs and SREs that only get noticed by management when they don't do their jobs).

During the launch of Windows 8, Msft's moonlighting policy was also part of their Windows App Store strategy: we were all heavily encouraged to make an "Windows Store App-app" so that SteveB could claim MS had N-many apps in its app-store, because that's how Leadership thought they could build credibility vs. Apple's established app store (of course, what actually ended-up happening was hundreds of cr-apps that were just WebView-wrappers over live websites).

In contrast, I understand Apple might have the worst moonlighting policy: I'm told that unless you directly work on WebKit or Darwin then you have to deactivate your GitHub account or else find yourself swiftly dragged onto the proverbial Trash.

在 HN 中查看
mort96 2026/04/28 03:16
我在挪威的几个软件开发人员合同中看到过这一点。我觉得它很卑鄙，并且总是在签署之前将其从任何合同中删除。我不明白为什么签订合同是合法的。我当然希望它无法执行。
I've seen it in a couple software developer contracts here in Norway. I find it despicable and have always gotten it removed from any contract before signing. I don't get why it's even legal to have in contracts. I certainly hope it's unenforceable.
在 HN 中查看
eloisant 2026/04/28 03:23
这取决于司法管辖区，它在挪威可能不合法或可执行，但至少在加利福尼亚州肯定是这样。

有一个著名的美泰起诉布拉茨的诉讼，理由是布拉茨的创造者在受雇于美泰期间就开始制作他的新娃娃。

我不知道它是如何结束的，但它并没有立即被驳回，他们在法庭上呆了几年。

It depends on the juridiction, it might not be legal or enforceable in Norway but it definitely is at least in California.

There is the famous lawsuit of Mattel suing Bratz, on the basis that the Bratz creator started to work on his new dolls while being employed by Mattel.

I'm not sure how it ended, but it wasn't dismissed right away and they spent years in court.

margalabargala 2026/04/28 03:50
> 有一个著名的美泰起诉布拉茨的诉讼，理由是布拉茨的创造者在受雇于美泰期间就开始制作他的新娃娃。

考虑到布拉茨是竞争对手，这至少是合理的。

如果布拉茨的创造者在一家制造水过滤器的公司工作时就开始研究它们，那是不合理的。

> There is the famous lawsuit of Mattel suing Bratz, on the basis that the Bratz creator started to work on his new dolls while being employed by Mattel.

That's at least reasonable considering Bratz is a competitor.

If the Bratz creator started working on them while working for a company that made water filters, that would not be reasonable.

drob518 2026/04/28 03:19

在 HN 中查看
tristor 2026/04/28 03:22
不幸的是，即使在非创意角色和领域，知识产权转让协议也很常见。 在美国，很多很多公司都有过于宽泛的雇佣协议，主要是因为他们知道很少有人会挑战它，而且对工人的法律保护基本上没有。 我个人永远不会签署未明确规定仅适用于工作时间和公司提供的设备的知识产权转让协议。 我用自己的时间用自己的设备做什么是我自己的事。
Unfortunately IP assignment agreements are very common, even in non-creative roles and fields. Many many many companies have overly-broad employment agreements in the US, mostly because they know few people will challenge it and that the legal protections for workers are basically nothing. I personally will never sign an IP assignment agreement that isn't explicitly scoped to apply only to work hours and company-provided equipment. What I do on my own time with my own equipment is my own business.
在 HN 中查看
paulddraper 2026/04/28 03:19
其实很常见。

这就是所谓的“知识产权的广泛分配”。一些司法管辖区不允许该条款。

当然，还有截然不同但主题相似的反兼职条款。

过度但常见。像大多数事情一样，律师会尽可能多地索取。

Pretty common actually.

It’s called “broad assignment of IP.” Some jurisdictions disallow that clause.

And then of course there is the distinct but thematically similar anti-moonlighting clause.

Overreaching but common. Like most things, lawyers will take as much as they can possibly get.

在 HN 中查看
zulux 2026/04/28 04:16
我讨厌工会，但我注意到它们只有在存在某种虐待时才会形成。善待员工，这不是问题。

另外，翻转层次结构：一家将员工放在第一位、将所有者的利润放在最后的企业通常可以为所有者带来大量利润。执行并不容易，需要智慧和努力——但这就是业主获得高额报酬的原因。

如果所有者无法破解它......那么这是一个技能问题。得到吧。

I hate unions, but I've noticed they only form when there's some sort of abuse. Treat employees well, and this isn't a problem.

Also, flip the hierarchy: a business that puts employees first and profits for owners last can often have a shit ton of profits for owners. Executing isn't easy and requires wisdom and work - but that's why owners are paid the big bucks.

If owners can't hack it... Then it's a skill issue. Get gud.

在 HN 中查看
nsingh2 2026/04/28 04:22
> 一家将员工放在第一位、将所有者的利润放在最后的企业通常可以为所有者带来巨额利润。

如果业主把利润放在第一位，他们可以赚 100 倍的钱，那他们为什么不这样做呢？出于自己内心的善良？

> a business that puts employees first and profits for owners last can often have a shit ton of profits for owners.

Owners can make 100x that shit ton if they put profits for owners first, so why wouldn’t they do that instead? Out of the goodness of their own hearts?

在 HN 中查看
monknomo 2026/04/28 04:45
对我来说，这个评论充满了矛盾。

当你认识到它们通常是虐待的结果时，你为什么要恨它们呢？ 我承认工会并不总是很棒；这些规则可能会适得其反，有时甚至具有限制性，但它们取决于工会成员，所以归根结底，它们是有某种原因的。

第二段——除了员工持股计划或员工所有的合作社之外，工会几乎是镇上唯一的游戏，除了祈祷并希望公司所有者、董事会或股票市场能够摆脱困境。 这可能是一个很大的提升。

To me, this comment is a bucket of contradictions.

Why do you hate them, when you recognize that they are general the result of abuse? I'll cop to unions not always being great; the rules can be counterproductive, or sometimes limiting, but they are up to the union members, so at the end of the day there is some kind of reason for them.

And the second paragraph - barring ESOP or employee-owned co-ops a union is pretty much the only game in town other than crossing your fingers and hoping the company owners, or board, or stock market are capable of pulling their head out of their ass. this can be a big lift.

darkwater 2026/04/28 04:22

glasss 2026/04/28 03:04
很高兴看到！我认为在大多数情况下工会应该是默认的。
Great to see! I think unions should be the default for most situations.
在 HN 中查看
satvikpendem 2026/04/28 03:21
我想知道为什么在美国，除了美国反工会情绪的典型原因（如法团主义、自力更生心态等）之外，这种情况没有发生在专门针对开发人员的科技领域（因为有 Alphabet 工人工会），尽管美国有许多工会，如 UAW、警察工会和教师工会等。

对于科技行业来说，这主要是一系列不同的原因，比如高工资，本身没有真正的不满，以及转移到其他公司的便利性，再加上工作都是虚拟的，所以如果工人只是在电脑上工作，公司没有理由不能外包到工会没有权力的另一个领域。后一个原因实际上正是 Netflix 大力投资韩国制作的原因。

I wonder why in America it doesn't happen in the tech sector for devs specifically (as there is Alphabet Workers Union), beyond the typical reasons of American anti union sentiment like corporatism, bootstrap mentality etc, despite which there are many unions in the US like UAW, police and teachers unions etc.

For tech, it's largely a different set of reasons, like high wages, no real grievances per se, and the ease of transferring to other companies, plus the work is all virtual so there is no reason why companies cannot outsource to another area where the union has no power, if the workers are just on their computers for work anyway. This latter reason is actually exactly why Netflix is investing heavily in South Korean productions.

satvikpendem 2026/04/28 04:50

在 HN 中查看
northern-lights 2026/04/28 05:30
工会的目的不是防止裁员和角色减少。这是为了确保公司已尽最大努力找到问题的另一种解决方案（他们正试图通过裁员来解决），并确保公司对他们的所有决定负责。
The point of a union is not prevent layoffs and role reduction in force. It is to make sure that the company has done its absolute best to find another solution to the problem (that they're trying to solve with layoffs) and ensures that the company is accountable for all their decisions.
在 HN 中查看
satvikpendem 2026/04/28 05:33

This just limits hiring in the first place as we see in European branches of (generally, American) tech companies. And regardless, what solutions can be found by the union?
在 HN 中查看
northern-lights 2026/04/28 05:48
它不以任何方式限制招聘。

至于工会提供什么解决方案——我已经解释过了。它们的存在是为了保护工人的利益。他们的存在不是为了解决公司的问题。这就是公司的工作。

It does not limit hiring in any way.

As for what solutions union provide - I have already explained. They exist to protect workers' interests. They do not exist to solve company's problems. That is the job of the company.

satvikpendem 2026/04/28 05:57

For hiring, I meant it in terms of a chilling effect in that companies will not hire as many people as they are much harder to fire.

You said "It is to make sure that the company has done its absolute best to find another solution to the problem," and I am asking, what solutions have unions "made sure" that the companies have done instead of layoffs? I am looking for concrete examples as I often hear this claim but I'm not sure what a union can do if the solution is layoffs, so my question is, what other solutions are there?

satvikpendem 2026/04/28 07:08

在 HN 中查看
sfink 2026/04/28 03:45
我认为其中一些原因是“联盟”在美国和（例如）欧盟有不同的含义。美国工会具有明显的敌对性，并且倾向于使用将短期工会利益优化至高于一切的策略（通常与资本主义有关！），使用边缘政策和价值获取策略。根据我的薄弱理解，欧盟工会的协作性明显更高，如果有利于公司或机构的健康，则更有可能做出妥协。

我天真的观点：在美国，工会就是要创造另一群混蛋来平衡现有的混蛋。在欧盟，至少有一些想法是“嘿，也许我们不应该都是混蛋？” （或者至少，并非总是如此。）

这并没有解决你的问题，即为什么它不会在科技领域发生，但也许我的轶事观点足够广泛，可以添加到你的（已经很好的）假设集中？

I think some of it is that "union" has a different meaning in the US vs (eg) the EU. US unions are explicitly adversarial and tend to use the strategy (usually associated with capitalism!) of optimizing for short-term union benefit above all else, using brinkmanship and value-capturing tactics. EU unions, according to my weak understanding, are significantly more collaborative and more likely to be amenable to compromise if it contributes to the health of the corporations or institutions.

My naive view: in the US, unions are all about creating another set of assholes to counterbalance the existing assholes. In the EU, there's at least some thought towards "hey, maybe we shouldn't all be assholes?" (Or at least, not all of the time.)

That doesn't address your question of why it doesn't happen in the tech sector, but perhaps my anecdotal opinion is widespread enough to be added to your (already good) set of hypotheses?

culi 2026/04/28 03:19
他们在欧洲大部分地区！瑞典、丹麦、芬兰、挪威、奥地利、比利时、冰岛、意大利等，甚至法国的覆盖率也超过80%

在 HN 中查看
zetanor 2026/04/28 03:37
据我了解，欧洲没有很多强制性的工会安排。

在加拿大，工会通常是全店范围的，没有选择退出的机制，这使得它们非常粘，如果它们能够保持足够的腐败或冷漠，它们就会变得更具掠夺性。我相信美国许多州都存在类似的问题，但这只是基于新闻和小说中对美国工会的描述。

I understand that Europe doesn't have many mandatory union arrangements.

In Canada, unions are often shop-wide with no mechanism to opt out, which makes them very sticky and allows them to grow predatory if they can maintain enough corruption or apathy. I'm led to believe that many US states have similar problems, but that's only based on how American unions are portrayed in news and fiction.

And what has that really bought them? The wages are laughable compared to the US where you'd THINK us non-unionized tech workers would be paid minimum wage. And the famous European healthcare and long vacations are more a function of the government than anything the unions have explicitly bargained for.

hungryhobbit 2026/04/28 04:08
从那时起，他们可能一直在衰落，但最近我要指出的是，当他们将《神秘博士》、《变形金刚》、《小马宝莉》和一大堆其他垃圾引入到游戏中时， 一款关于巫师在奇幻世界中战斗的游戏。

在短期内，所有特许经营权都被证明非常受欢迎（就像《双周》一样），但从长远来看，我认为这意味着游戏的死亡（就像《双周》的发展方向一样）。

They might have been declining since then, but more recently I'd point to when they introduced Dr. Who, Transformers, My Little Pony, and a whole bunch of other crap into a game about wizards fighting in a fantasy world.

In the short term all the franchises have proved incredibly popular (just like in Fortnight), but in the long run I think it means the death of the game (just like where Fortnight is headed).

nonethewiser 2026/04/28 03:44

D&D is famously not more popular than ever and is one of the reasons why so many OSR's have exploded in popularity over the past 5-10 years.
在 HN 中查看
1attice 2026/04/28 03:58
OSR 复兴确实是事实， 和 《龙与地下城》比以往任何时候都更受欢迎——只是拥有更广泛和文化规范的受众。

另外：是的，孩之宝做了一些狗屎，但他们也不得不软化并抄送他们的 SRD。这一切都发生在几年前。

我是作为一名忠实的探路者玩家说这番话的，但我还没有找到重返 D&D 的充分理由（PF2e 就足够好了）。 D&D 正在做数字，没有人关心我们这些书呆子因为过去十年的淡茶而继续前进。

现在是他们的房子了，没问题。

It is both true that there has been an OSR revival, and that D&D is more popular than ever -- just with a much broader and culturally normative audience.

Also: Yes, Hasbro did some shit, but they also had to relent and CC their SRD. This all happened years ago.

I say this as a committed Pathfinder player who hasn't seen a good reason to return to D&D (PF2e is good enough). D&D is doing numbers and no one cares that us nerds moved on because of weak tea from the previous decade.

It's their house now, and it's fine.

在 HN 中查看
hellcow 2026/04/28 03:44
不久前，为了回应 WOTC（应孩之宝的要求），《D&D》发生了大规模的玩家反抗，试图获得玩家在《D&D》宇宙中创造的一切的所有权，这违背了他们对所有创作者和玩家的长期承诺。

几位支持玩家权益的《D&D》高层人士也在此时辞职。

WOTC/孩之宝在这种压力下态度软化，但桥梁已经被烧毁了。从那以后我的团队就再没有玩过 D&D 战役。我们改用 Pathfinder 2e，发现它是一个更好的系统。

D&D had a massive player revolt a while back in response to WOTC (at the requirement of Hasbro) trying to take ownership over everything players create in the D&D universe, breaking their long-standing promise to all of those creators and players.

Several of the top D&D people who were advocating for players resigned around this time.

WOTC/Hasbro since relented under this pressure, but the bridge was already burned. My group hasn't played a D&D campaign since. We switched to Pathfinder 2e and found that it was a better system altogether.

ddtaylor 2026/04/28 08:12

受欢迎程度并不是一个可靠的衡量标准。

Short form brain rot and fentanyl are more popular than ever.

Popularity is not a reliable metric.

bena 2026/04/28 04:23
那是1999年。《入侵》就在那一年上映。依尼翠、秘罗地、拉尼卡等设定全部出炉 后 孩之宝的收购。

迄今为止，每一年似乎都是最畅销的一年。 《万智牌》是孩之宝的主要收入来源。

看，我不喜欢他们现在每年生产的大量产品。但我意识到，从销量来看，我显然属于少数派。

That was 1999. Invasion came out that year. Innistrad, Mirrodin, Ravnica, and other settings all came out after Hasbro's acquisition.

Every year seems to be the best-selling year so far. Magic: the Gathering is Hasbro's primary revenue source.

And look, I don't like the sheer amount of product they're pumping out every year now. But I realize I'm clearly in the minority opinion based on sales.

在 HN 中查看
charcircuit 2026/04/28 05:12
这是一种癌症，需要通过解雇他们来尽快解决。这是一种意识形态上的有毒物质，不应该让它渗透进来。

>裁员保护

当市场发生变化且项目不再可行时，企业需要有能力解雇员工。

>远程工作保护

数据显示，在竞争环境（例如游戏）中，面对面交流的好处已得到证实，每一项优势都很重要。

>生成式人工智能保护

人工智能正在彻底改变所有行业。如果你拒绝用它来扰乱自己，别人就会这么做。

这些要求对公司来说非常危险，不应该被考虑。

This is a cancer that needs to be addressed as quickly as possible by firing them all. It is a toxic ideologically that should not be allowed to permeate.

>Layoff Protections

Businesses need the ability to layoff employees when markets change and projects are no longer viable.

>Remote Work Protections

Data has shown proven benefits of in person time and in a competitive environment such as gaming every advantage matters.

>Generative AI Protections

AI is revolutionizing all industries. If you refuse to use it to disrupt yourself someone else will.

These demands are very dangerous to the company and should not be considered.

在 HN 中查看
DonaldPShimoda 2026/04/28 05:21
> 裁员保护

公司通常希望员工提前通知终止雇佣关系，但雇主并不认为员工有义务给予回报。这是不合理的。

> 远程工作保护

当员工获得灵活性时，许多公司的生产力都得到了提高。相反的说法往往得不到管理职位人员精心挑选的结果之外的实际数据的支持。

> 生成式人工智能保护

MTG 是一种创造性的努力，其产出受版权保护。到目前为止（值得庆幸的是）版权只有在媒体由人类制作时才可执行。防止使用生成式人工智能不仅有利于工人的长期生产力，而且也符合法律目的。

---

我想也许你应该停止舔靴子并开始理解为什么我们的社会今天运转。提示一下，你曾经享受过的每一项保护（每周工作 40 小时、养老金或退休基金、健康保险保证、强制性带薪休假、健康和安全法规等）都是由公司认为不比利润更重要的人用鲜血换来的。工会是工人确保自身安全的基本能力。令人震惊的是，我认为依赖那些想要加入工会的从事这些工作的人的解释比网上一些随机的人认为“不等等，但想想可怜的濒临灭绝的公司！:(”更合理。

> Layoff Protections

Companies often expect employees to give advance notice of termination of employment, but employers do not feel beholden to their employees to reciprocate. This is unreasonable.

> Remote Work Protections

Many companies showed improved productivity when people were given flexibility. Claims to the contrary are often not backed up by actual data outside of cherry-picked results chosen by those in managerial positions.

> Generative AI Protections

MTG is a creative endeavor, and its output is protected by copyright. Copyright is, so far (thankfully), only enforceable when the media is produced by humans. Protections against the use of generative AI are not only good for workers' long-term productivity, but also for legal purposes.

---

I think maybe you should stop licking boots and start understanding why any of our society works today at all. As a hint, every protection you have ever enjoyed (40-hour work weeks, pensions or retirement funds, health insurance guarantees, mandatory paid time off, health and safety regulations, and so on) was paid for in blood by someone a company decided was not more important than profit. Unions are a fundamental capability by workers to ensure their own safety. Shockingly, I think it more reasonable to rely on the interpretations of those working in these jobs who want to unionize than some random person online who thinks "no wait, but think of the poor endangered company! :("

在 HN 中查看
charcircuit 2026/04/28 06:05
>但雇主并不觉得有义务让雇员给予回报。

在像 WotC 这样的公司，您将获得超过 2 周工资的遣散费，这比员工提前 2 周通知的金额还要多。还有像加利福尼亚州的 WARN 这样的情况，在裁员时需要提前两周以上的通知。

>当赋予员工灵活性时，许多公司的生产力都得到了提高。

但个人生产力往往不是瓶颈。目标应该是使流程富有成效且高效。

>仅当媒体由人类制作时才可执行

好莱坞多年来一直使用人工智能和其他机器制作的图形来实现特效，但这并不妨碍工作室拥有电影的版权。

>停止舔靴子

承认公司为社会和投资者带来了多少价值，并不需要舔靴子或认为他们是一家“可怜的濒临灭绝的公司”。

>but employers do not feel beholden to their employees to reciprocate.

At a company like WotC you will get more than 2 weeks of pay worth of severance which is more than the employee equivalent of providing 2 weeks notice. There is also things like WARN in California which require much more than a 2 weeks notice when a doing a layoff.

>Many companies showed improved productivity when people were given flexibility.

Except that individual productivity is very often not the bottleneck. The goal should be making the process productive and efficient.

>only enforceable when the media is produced by humans

Hollywood has used AI and other machine produced graphics for special effects for years and that does not prevent the studio from having copyright over the movie.

>stop licking boots

Acknowledging how much value companies bring to both society and investors does not require licking boots or thinking they are a "poor endangered company."

在 HN 中查看
jujube3 2026/04/28 05:56 

  Companies often expect employees to give advance
  notice of termination of employment, but employers
  do not feel beholden to their employees to
  reciprocate. This is unreasonable.
工人可以在没有通知期的情况下离职，并且不会承担任何法律后果。传统的两周通知期是出于礼貌，并非法律要求。与此同时，雇主通常对一次可以解雇多少工人有广泛的法律限制（例如，由于《警告法案》）。解雇某些工人（例如休伤残假的工人）可能是不可能的。雇主向被解雇或下岗的员工支付遣散费是传统做法，有时这可能是法律要求的。

没有人可以强迫工人工作，但雇主常常被迫雇用他们不愿意雇用的人。也许有时这是有道理的，但实际上与您想要声明的完全相反。

  Many companies showed improved productivity when
  people were given flexibility. Claims to the contrary
  are often not backed up by actual data outside of
  cherry-picked results chosen by those in managerial
  positions.
如果远程工作如此高效，那么管理层应该在没有任何强制的情况下选择远程工作。如果你想要更多的好处，那就诚实一点，不要声称你比管理层更了解什么是高效的。

  MTG is a creative endeavor, and its output is
  protected by copyright. Copyright is, so far
  (thankfully), only enforceable when the media
  is produced by humans. Protections against the
  use of generative AI are not only good for 
  workers' long-term productivity, but also 
  for legal purposes
版权适用于部分由人工智能制作、部分由人类制作的作品。拒绝使用其他人正在使用的工具来提高生产力只会让公司变成恐龙。

  I think maybe you should stop licking boots and
  start understanding why any of our society works
  today at all.
工会也是一个公司，为其官员赚取利润。也许你应该停止舔它的靴子？

  As a hint, every protection you have
  ever enjoyed (40-hour work weeks, pensions or retirement
  funds, health insurance guarantees, mandatory paid time
  off, health and safety regulations, and so on) was paid
  for in blood by someone a company decided was not more
  important than profit. Unions are a fundamental  capability
  by workers to ensure their own safety. Shockingly, I think
  it more reasonable to rely on the interpretations of those
  working in these jobs who want to unionize than some random
  person online who thinks "no wait, but think of the poor
  endangered company! :("
我不是任何工会的成员。我享受的保护是因为适用于每个人的法律，也是因为经济状况。如果经济形势发生变化，无论工会或政府做什么，我都会被解雇，就像汽车兴起后马鞍制造商被解雇一样。

  Companies often expect employees to give advance
  notice of termination of employment, but employers
  do not feel beholden to their employees to
  reciprocate. This is unreasonable.
Workers can leave their jobs with no notice period at all and suffer no legal consequences. The traditional two weeks notice period is a courtesy and not legally required. Meanwhile, employers often have extensive legal limitations on how many workers they can fire at a time (for example, because of the WARN act). It may be impossible to fire some workers (for example, those on disability leave). It's traditional for employers to pay severance to employees that are fired or laid off, and this may sometimes be legally required.

Nobody can force a worker to work, but an employer is often forced to employ someone they'd rather not. Maybe sometimes this makes sense, but it's actually the exact opposite of what you're trying to claim.

  Many companies showed improved productivity when
  people were given flexibility. Claims to the contrary
  are often not backed up by actual data outside of
  cherry-picked results chosen by those in managerial
  positions.
If remove work is so productive, then management should choose remote work without any coercion. If you want more benefits, then just be honest and don't make claims that you know better than management about what is productive.

  MTG is a creative endeavor, and its output is
  protected by copyright. Copyright is, so far
  (thankfully), only enforceable when the media
  is produced by humans. Protections against the
  use of generative AI are not only good for 
  workers' long-term productivity, but also 
  for legal purposes
Copyright applies to works that are partially produced by AI and partially produced by humans. Refusing to use the tools that everyone else is using to be more productive is just going to make companies into dinosaurs.

  I think maybe you should stop licking boots and
  start understanding why any of our society works
  today at all.
The union is also a corporation, which makes profit for its officers. Maybe you should stop licking its boots?

  As a hint, every protection you have
  ever enjoyed (40-hour work weeks, pensions or retirement
  funds, health insurance guarantees, mandatory paid time
  off, health and safety regulations, and so on) was paid
  for in blood by someone a company decided was not more
  important than profit. Unions are a fundamental  capability
  by workers to ensure their own safety. Shockingly, I think
  it more reasonable to rely on the interpretations of those
  working in these jobs who want to unionize than some random
  person online who thinks "no wait, but think of the poor
  endangered company! :("
I'm not a member of any union. The protections I enjoy are because of the law, which applies to everyone, and because of the economic situation. If the economic situation changes, I will be fired no matter what unions or governments do, the same way saddle-makers were after the rise of the automobile.

perrohunter 2026/04/28 03:34
我认为有些职业工会是有意义的，比如飞行员和教师，因为30年经验的教师可以被2年经验的教师取代，所以我们需要工会来保护经验丰富的教师或飞行员，但是当谈到MTG Arena的作用时，我认为现有的工会没有太大价值，也许有人可以提供一些有价值的见解
I think there are some professions where Unions make sense, like for Pilots and Teachers, as a 30 years of experience teacher can be replaced with a 2 years of experience teacher, so we need unions to protect the experienced Teacher or Pilot, but when it comes to what MTG Arena does, I dont see much value on the union existing, perhaps someone can provide some valuable insight
在 HN 中查看
looneysquash 2026/04/28 03:47
你工作的公司在谈判中几乎总是比你拥有更大的权力。 （对于一些假设的“你”。）

公司越大，通常拥有的权力就越大。

如果您想赚更多的钱或获得更好的福利或以其他方式谈判更好的合同，您需要更多的杠杆。

加入工会是通过与同事一起谈判而不是单独谈判来获得更多谈判权力的一种方式。

它还可以更轻松地解决安全和公平等跨领域问题。

The company you work for almost always has more power in negotiations than you do. (For some hypothetical "you".)

The bigger the company is, the more power they have typically.

If you want to make more money or get better benefits or otherwise negotiate a better contract, you need more leverage.

Unionizing is one way to gain more negotiation power by negotiating together with your co-workers instead of individually.

It also makes it easier to address cross cutting concerns like safety and fairness.

ryandrake 2026/04/28 03:56

A lot of HN repliers think that they, out of thousands of other employees, are that One Captain Of Industry that has sufficient bargaining and negotiating power to stand toe to toe with a huge employer. And therefore, a union could never help tech employees as a class.

robrenaud 2026/04/28 04:05

例如，他们需要像纽约市公立学校那样的所谓“橡胶室”，在那里，老师们在等待仲裁期间什么也不做，他们拿薪水。

Eg, that they necessitated so called "rubber rooms" like these in the NYC public schools, where teachers got paid to do nothing while waiting on arbitration.

threetonesun 2026/04/28 04:12
我怀疑你也会发现很多人赞成坏警察如何受到警察工会的保护。 At least in the US I'd much rather a broad social net so my health care and retirement weren't so directly tied to my job than a union specific to my trade.
I doubt you'll find many people in favor of how bad cops get protected by police unions either. At least in the US I'd much rather a broad social net so my health care and retirement weren't so directly tied to my job than a union specific to my trade.
在 HN 中查看
michaelt 2026/04/28 04:52
我看到的许多美国科技员工的薪资数字都相当疯狂 [1] - 20 万美元、40 万美元的总薪酬，甚至没有获得“高级”职位。

这是一个家庭收入中位数为 8.4 万美元的国家 [2]

我认为，如果有人带回家的钱是邻居和亲人的 4.7 倍，他们会觉得自己在讨价还价和谈判方面做得很好，这是可以理解的。

从各方面来看，游戏行业的人们的工资和工作条件都很差，所以我可以 110% 理解他们为什么要成立工会。

[1] https://www.levels.fyi/?tab=levels&compare=Google%2CMeta%2CA... [2] https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/MEHOINUSA672N

A lot of the salary figures I see thrown around for tech employees in the US are pretty wild [1] - $200k, $400k total compensation without even getting 'Senior' in the job title.

That's in a country with a median household income of $84k [2]

I think it's understandable why someone would feel they were doing well at bargaining and negotiating if they were taking home 4.7x as much as their neighbours and loved ones.

Folks in the games industry by all accounts have really shitty pay and working conditions so I can 110% understand why they'd unionise.

[1] https://www.levels.fyi/?tab=levels&compare=Google%2CMeta%2CA... [2] https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/MEHOINUSA672N

在 HN 中查看
ryandrake 2026/04/28 05:28
薪资只是组织可以帮助解决的众多工作场所问题之一。那些赚 40 万美元的美国科技人员，这对他们来说太棒了！但他们的心理健康/倦怠状况如何？他们的健康保险有多好？他们有足够的假期或与家人在一起的时间吗？他们通常每天工作 12 小时以上吗？他们有周末吗？他们是否想在家工作但无法实现？兼职或从事业余项目的许可怎么样？他们在工作中是否拥有令人满意的自主权，或者他们只是在翻阅 JIRA 票证？

如果我们允许的话，集体谈判的力量可以帮助解决所有这些问题。 It doesn't just stop at compensation.

Salary is only one of many workplace issues that organizing can help with. Those US tech people who make $400k, that's great for them! But how is their mental health / burnout? How good is their health insurance? Are they getting enough vacation time or time with their families? Are they regularly expected to work 12+ hour days? Do they have their weekends? Do they want to Work From Home but can't get it? What about permission to moonlight or work on side projects? Do they have satisfying autonomy at work or are they just churning through JIRA tickets?

Collective negotiation power can help with all of these, if we let it. It doesn't just stop at compensation.

在 HN 中查看
satvikpendem 2026/04/28 06:07
一般来说，大型科技工人的工作非常舒适，有良好的保险和工作条件，许多人每周工作时间仅超过 30 小时，更不用说 40 小时了。这就是为什么许多人看不到工会的好处，因为工会能给他们带来什么？
Generally big tech workers have very cushy jobs with excellent insurance and working conditions with many barely working over 30 hours a week, much less 40. It is why many do not see the benefits of a union because what could a union possibly give them?
在 HN 中查看
ryandrake 2026/04/28 06:54
这远非常态。如果这对少数地区、少数公司的少数员工来说是这样的话，那并不意味着对每个人来说都是如此。我不知道有哪个员工认为工作中绝对没有什么可以做得更好的。
This is far from the norm. If it's true for a small number of employees at a small number of companies in a small number of regions, that doesn't mean it's true for everyone. I don't know a single employee who thinks absolutely nothing could be made better at work.
在 HN 中查看
satvikpendem 2026/04/28 06:57
当然，每个人都有他们想要做得更好的东西，但我不确定工会是可以解决这些问题的工具，其中很多都是公司特有的管理问题。
Sure, everyone has something they'd want to make better but I'm not sure a union is the tool that can fix them, lots of them are management issues endemic to the company.
在 HN 中查看
satvikpendem 2026/04/28 06:08
因为通常他们可以，因为软件工程师在过去的几十年里对他们的雇主拥有巨大的影响力，以至于像这样的字面模仿 硅谷 是由他们轻松的工作组成的。也许现在人工智能正在改变这种情况，但我对此表示怀疑。
Because generally they can, as software engineers had huge leverage over their employers for the past few decades, so much so that literal parodies like Silicon Valley were made of the cushiness of their jobs. Maybe that is changing now with AI but I doubt it.
在 HN 中查看
lovich 2026/04/28 04:51
一旦我们谈到“我可以自己协商加薪”时，我就收到了很多来自这些类型的询问，询问他们实际上尝试过多少次谈判，但成功的次数却少得多。
I have gotten a lot of mileage asking from these types once we get to the “I can negotiate my own raises” by asking them how many times they’ve actually tried negotiating much less succeeded.
在 HN 中查看
Lonestar1440 2026/04/28 04:04
这里的一个重要假设是，工会的利益实际上与您，工人是一致的。

如果不是，那么你就陷入了两个强大的、不负责任的实体之间。你 有 毕竟加入联盟。我看到很多教育界人士认为工会只是存在，并没有真正帮助他们（他们的雇主也相当同情）。

当它们存在时，工人当然会受益。医疗保健和航空公司员工似乎属于这一阵营。

The big assumption here is that the Union's interests are, in fact, aligned with You, the Worker.

If they're not, then you're just caught between two powerful, unaccountable entities. You have to join the Union, after all. I see a lot of folks in Education who feel that the Union simply Exists and does not really help them (their employers being rather sympathetic as well).

When they are, of course the Worker benefits. Healthcare and Airline employees seem to fall into this camp.

在 HN 中查看
glasss 2026/04/28 04:11
我认为平均而言，一个更安全的假设是，由你的同事组成的工会比公司所有者更符合你的利益。

我所有的老师朋友都承认他们的工会有缺陷，但也非常感激有强有力的合同、福利，以及当学校系统试图压垮他们时愿意为他们而战的人们。

I think on average it's a safer assumption that the union made up of your peers are more aligned with your interests than the owners of the company are.

All of my friends that are teachers do admit their union has flaws, but also are very grateful to have strong contracts, benefits, and people willing to fight for them when the school system tries to screw them over.

在 HN 中查看
baconsunday 2026/04/28 04:22
然而，实际存在的技术工会的情况真的是这样吗？ Historically, the people pushing for tech unionization were doing it for ideological reasons, not in response to the relatively recent layoffs, etc. and you can see this in their leadership.

我对他们做了一个非常简单的试金石：他们是否反对 H1B 招聘？当詹姆斯·达莫尔（James Damore）因患有自闭症而被谷歌开除时，他们会为他辩护吗？我认为他们中的许多人的答案是否定的。

Is that actually the case for the tech unions that actually exist, though? Historically, the people pushing for tech unionization were doing it for ideological reasons, not in response to the relatively recent layoffs, etc. and you can see this in their leadership.

I have a pretty simple litmus test for them: are they opposed to H1B hiring, and would they have defended James Damore when he got ousted from Google for basically being autistic? I think the answer for many of them is a resounding no.

在 HN 中查看
lovich 2026/04/28 04:49
如果你提到詹姆斯·达莫尔，听起来你在意识形态上也同样反对。

关于反对现在实施的 H1B，我同意你的观点，但在一个假设的技术工会世界中，詹姆斯·达莫尔 (James Damore) 仍然会主张解雇大批工会成员。他是厌恶女性，而不是“基本上是自闭症”。

It sounds like you’re just as ideologically in your opposition if you’re bringing up James Damore.

On opposing H1B as they are implemented now I agree with you, but in a hypothetical world with tech unions James Damore would still be advocating for large swathes of fellow union members to be removed. He was being misogynistic not “basically being autistic”.

在 HN 中查看
baconsunday 2026/04/28 05:29
我认为达​​莫尔在内部撰写和发布它并且不同意其中的部分内容是愚蠢的，但我看到的对他的宣言的几乎所有反应都是对它的第三手歪曲或故意误读。 （例如，他提到有关神经质的人口统计数据被解释为他说他的女同事很神经质）。我认为你的回复是一个完美的例子：他 不是 他主张将女性“剔除”，他认为 DEI 试图实现男女 50/50 平衡的努力从根本上来说是错误的，因为想要从事科技行业的女性并不像男性那么多。

但无论你是否同意他的观点，你都应该同意这样的观点：工会的主要工作之一就是为他提供公平的辩护，无论工会领导层是否喜欢他。我认为任何技术工会都不会这样做。

编辑：让我这样说。假设您在内部政治论坛上发帖表示您反对 H1B 计划，然后因为人们声称您讨厌移民并希望您的同事被驱逐出境而被解雇。你认为这些工会会保护你吗？

I think that Damore was stupid for writing and posting it internally and disagree with portions of it, but almost all of the reactions I saw to his manifesto were to third-hand misrepresentations of it or willful misreadings of it. (e.g. him referring to population statistics on neuroticism being interpreted as him saying that his female coworkers were neurotic). I think your reply is the perfect example: he wasn't advocating for women to be 'removed', he was arguing that DEI efforts to try to get a 50/50 male/female balance are fundamentally misguided because not as many women want to work in tech as men.

But whether or not you agree with him, you should agree with the idea that one of the primary jobs of a union would have been to give him a fair defense regardless of whether the union leadership likes him or not. I don't think any of the tech unions would do that.

edit: Let me put it this way. Suppose you make an post in an internal politics discussion forum saying that you oppose the H1B program as it is, and then get fired because people claim that you hate immigrants and want your fellow coworkers to be deported. Do you think these unions would defend you?

在 HN 中查看
defrost 2026/04/28 07:26
> 假设您在内部政治讨论论坛上发帖表示您反对 H1B 计划，然后因为人们声称您讨厌移民并希望您的同事被驱逐出境而被解雇。你认为这些工会会保护你吗？

在澳大利亚长期存在的教师工会的现实案例中（它们因州而异），在如此肤浅的结构的基础上回答这样的问题实际上是不可能的。

答案都是肯定的 和 否 - 在任何具体情况下，都会考虑个人情况 - 例如：如所规定的那样 是的，他们辩护，但在大多数现实情况中，实地情况要复杂得多，一群同行工会成员同事大声疾呼不辩护的情况并不少见。

> Suppose you make an post in an internal politics discussion forum saying that you oppose the H1B program as it is, and then get fired because people claim that you hate immigrants and want your fellow coworkers to be deported. Do you think these unions would defend you?

In the real world case of long standing Teachers Unions in Australia (they vary by state) it is literally impossible to answer such a question on the basis of such a shallow construct.

The answer is both Yes and No - in any specific case the individual circumstances would be looked at - eg: as laid out Yes, they defend, however in most IRL cases the circumstances on the ground are far more complex, and it wouldn't be uncommon for a bunch of fellow peer union member coworkers to speak up in favour of not defending.

在 HN 中查看
lovich 2026/04/28 09:02
> 让我这样说吧。假设您在内部政治论坛上发帖表示您反对 H1B 计划，然后因为人们声称您讨厌移民并希望您的同事被驱逐出境而被解雇。你认为这些工会会保护你吗？

是的。但如果我声称 H1B 持有者在生物学上不愿具备开发软件的能力，我就不会这么做。

达莫尔只是一个厌恶女性的人。

> Let me put it this way. Suppose you make an post in an internal politics discussion forum saying that you oppose the H1B program as it is, and then get fired because people claim that you hate immigrants and want your fellow coworkers to be deported. Do you think these unions would defend you?

Yea. But if I made a claim that h1b holders were biologically disinclined to not be as capable of doing software, I wouldn’t.

Damore was just a misogynist.

在 HN 中查看
Lonestar1440 2026/04/28 07:13
对于很多老师来说，没有“公司”。工会与国家和选民民主选择公务员的薪酬结构进行英勇斗争。

此外，我认为此类联盟的存在可以激励选民 不是 直接向这些仆人分配更多的工资和福利。 Mayors and Governors know that it's always going to be a "Union game" and all they can do is negotiate - even when they're Progressives who actually want to pay teachers well.

当警察而不是公立学校老师时，情况会变得更糟。

根据我的经验，对“利益”的大假设会导致糟糕的分析。

In the case of many teachers, there is no "company". The union fights valiantly against the State, and against the pay structure Voters democratically select for their public servants.

Further, I posit that existence of such Unions serves as an incentive for voters not to simply assign more pay and benefits to such servants directly. Mayors and Governors know that it's always going to be a "Union game" and all they can do is negotiate - even when they're Progressives who actually want to pay teachers well.

It just gets worse when it's Cops instead of State School teachers.

Big assumption about "interests" lead to bad analysis, in my experience.

kaoD 2026/04/28 05:17
> 毕竟你必须加入联盟

呃，怎么样？这可能是一个国家的事情，但你不是 有 加入我国的任何工会。如果他们代表你的利益，你就这样做。大公司有多个相互竞争的工会，而无政府主义者（他们拒绝国家补贴并且完全自筹资金）非常擅长他们所做的事情。

如果你 有 加入工会本质上不就是一种骗局吗？

> You have to join the Union, after all

Uh, how? This might be a country thing but you don't have to join any union in my country. You do, if they represent your interests. Big companies have multiple, competing unions, and the anarchists (which refuse state subsidies and are fully self-funded) are pretty good at what they do.

If you have to join a union isn't that essentially a racket?

Lonestar1440 2026/04/28 07:06

none2585 2026/04/28 06:05

在 HN 中查看
nonethewiser 2026/04/28 03:53
您刚才描述了为什么工人应该想要成立工会。但这并不是决定工会是否会在行业中流行的因素。这是由行业结构决定的。如果事情并不重要，并且有大量替代者等待取代你的工作，工会就不会大规模兴起。
You just described why workers should want a union. But that's not what determines whether or not unions will catch on in the industry. Thats determined by the structure of the industry. If its not critical and there are plenty of replacements waiting to take your job, unionization just wont take off on a large scale.
在 HN 中查看
matthewkayin 2026/04/28 03:45
一般来说，制作这些游戏的工作人员实际上很关心游戏，并希望为玩家提供良好的体验。然而，高管们只关心他们能从产品中赚多少钱。

拥有工会（假设工会运作良好），

- 确保为客户提供更好的产品

- 确保工人有更好的工作条件

- 确保工人获得更好的工资和福利（至少在编程职位上，与其他行业的开发人员相比，游戏行业的工资普遍偏低）

- 提供保护，防止不当解雇和裁员

我很想知道为什么你认为工会对这些人没有好处。

Well generally the workers who make these games actually care about the game and want to make a good experience for the players. The execs however, only care about how much money they can make off of the product.

Having a union (assuming that the union is run well),

- ensures a better product for customers

- ensures better working conditions for workers

- ensures better pay and benefits from workers (at least in programming roles, the games industry is generally underpaid compared to developers in other industries)

- provides protections against undue firings and layoffs

I would be curious to know why you don't think a union would be good for these people.

我可以说的内容不多 https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trade_union

Well, unions do not exist to keep people entrenched on their jobs. That perspective is propaganda (by you-know-who).
